quote: Jack Ketch stands there, elbows locked, for all the world like a carpenter
subject: Jack Ketch
note: Jack Ketch was a famous English executioner. Providing context on his notoriety would enhance the reader's understanding of the grim scene.

quote: On Sir Isaac Newton’s temperature scale, where freezing is zero
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Newton proposed a temperature scale, though it's not the one commonly used today. This is a good place to link to the existing page.

quote: If Herr Fahrenheit were here with one of his new quicksilver-filled
subject: Fahrenheit
note: Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit was a physicist who invented the mercury thermometer and the Fahrenheit scale. A brief introduction would be helpful.

quote: books in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin pocked with the occult symbols of Alchemists
subject: Alchemists
note: Alchemy was a proto-scientific tradition. This is a good place to link to the existing page.

quote: French or Spanish galleons that might trespass on the bay
subject: French or Spanish galleons
note: This alludes to the ongoing conflicts between England, France, and Spain in the early 18th century, specifically Queen Anne's War (the North American theater of the War of the Spanish Succession). Providing this context would clarify the threat.

quote: what Leibniz would call the Ordinate
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: Leibniz was a prominent philosopher and mathematician. This is a good place to link to the existing page.

quote: witches are being hanged five minutes’ walk away
subject: witch trials
note: Briefly explain the history of witch trials in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, including the Salem Witch Trials, and their cultural context.

quote: Back in America and among Puritans
subject: Puritans
note: Provide a brief overview of Puritan beliefs and their historical context in 17th-century England and America.

quote: A Peace has been signed at Utrecht.
subject: Treaty of Utrecht
note: Explain the Treaty of Utrecht and its significance in ending the War of the Spanish Succession, including the territorial changes and agreements made.

quote: France gets Spain. Austria gets the Spanish Netherlands.
subject: territorial changes after Treaty of Utrecht
note: Explain the territorial changes and their significance in the European balance of power.

quote: We get Gibraltar, Newfoundland, St. Kitts, and—
subject: British gains after Treaty of Utrecht
note: Explain the significance of these territorial gains for Great Britain, particularly in terms of trade and naval power.

quote: the slave trade.
subject: Asiento
note: Explain the Asiento de Negros, its history, and its role in the transatlantic slave trade.

quote: War of the Spanish Succession,” says Ben, “whose cause was the death in Madrid of King Carlos the Sufferer.
subject: War of the Spanish Succession
note: Explain the causes and major players in the War of the Spanish Succession, including the role of Charles II of Spain (Carlos the Sufferer).

quote: The Glorious Revolution!
subject: Glorious Revolution
note: Explain the Glorious Revolution in England, including the deposition of James II and the accession of William and Mary.

quote: William and Mary!
subject: William and Mary
note: Provide brief biographies of William III and Mary II and their role as joint monarchs of England, Scotland, and Ireland.

quote: The Civil War,” says Ben.
subject: English Civil War
note: Explain the English Civil War, including the conflict between Royalists and Parliamentarians, and its impact on English society.

quote: the Restoration
subject: Restoration
note: Explain the Restoration of the monarchy in England, including the return of Charles II and the changes in English society and politics.

quote: Archbishop Laud
subject: Archbishop Laud
note: Provide a brief biography of William Laud and his role as Archbishop of Canterbury, including his controversial religious policies and persecution of Puritans.

quote: Charles the First. The Puritans paid him back by chopping off the head of that same Charles in Charing Cross, in the year of our lord sixteen hundred and forty-nine.
subject: Charles I
note: Explain the reign of Charles I, his conflicts with Parliament, and his execution during the English Civil War.

quote: Cromwell
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: Provide a brief biography of Oliver Cromwell and his role as Lord Protector of England during the Interregnum.

quote: He watched the head of King Charles the First being chopped off.
subject: Charles I
note: Charles I was King of England, Scotland, and Ireland. His execution in 1649 was a pivotal event in English history.

quote: Archbishop Laud had caused his ears and his nose to be cut off in Star Chamber
subject: Star Chamber
note: The Star Chamber was an English court of law that became notorious for its secret and arbitrary proceedings, often used by Charles I to suppress dissent.

quote: the one I seek had an excellent view of the beheading, for he was sitting upon his father’s shoulders.
subject: Puritans
note: Puritans were a religious group who sought to purify the Church of England. They played a significant role in the English Civil War and the colonization of America.

quote: coins that are being stamped out by Sir Isaac Newton in the Royal Mint at the Tower of London.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Isaac Newton was a highly influential physicist, mathematician, and natural philosopher. He served as Warden and Master of the Royal Mint.

quote: coins that are being stamped out by Sir Isaac Newton in the Royal Mint at the Tower of London.
subject: Royal Mint
note: The Royal Mint is the official mint of the United Kingdom, responsible for producing coins. Its location at the Tower of London is historically significant.

quote: coins that are being stamped out by Sir Isaac Newton in the Royal Mint at the Tower of London.
subject: Tower of London
note: The Tower of London is a historic castle that has served as a royal palace, prison, and treasury, among other things. The Royal Mint was located there for centuries.

quote: The King of Spain’s coat of arms stares back at him, stamped in silver
subject: King of Spain
note: During this period, the King of Spain controlled vast territories in the Americas, and Spanish silver coins were a major form of international currency.

quote: a passage down to the Caribbean to go a-pirating on the Spanish Main.
subject: Pirates
note: The Spanish Main refers to the mainland coast of the Spanish Empire in the Americas, a region heavily targeted by pirates.

quote: tracing out fading and flattening conic sections that Sir Isaac could probably work out in his head.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Newton's work in mathematics, including his understanding of conic sections, was foundational to his theories of physics and astronomy.

quote: The Hypothesis of Vortices is pressed with many difficulties.
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: The Hypothesis of Vortices was a theory proposed by René Descartes to explain planetary motion. Newton's theory of gravity eventually superseded it.

quote: The slave is very dark, and the arms of the King of Spain are branded into his left shoulder, and so he is probably Angolan.
subject: Slavery
note: The branding of slaves with the arms of the King of Spain was a common practice to mark ownership and indicate that duties had been paid. Angola was a major source of slaves for the Spanish colonies.

quote: his brethren in London (and that is a very good assumption)
subject: Religious conflict in London
note: This alludes to the ongoing religious tensions and conflicts in London, particularly between Puritans and the established Church of England. Some context on the political and social climate of religious dissent would be helpful.

quote: thousands of slaves—some Irish, the rest Angolan
subject: Irish slavery
note: The reference to Irish slaves in New York requires some historical context. While the transatlantic slave trade primarily involved Africans, there was also a history of Irish people being enslaved, particularly during the 17th century. Providing some background on this would be beneficial.

quote: halcyon days of Cathedral-sacking and King-killing
subject: English Civil War
note: This refers to the English Civil War and the execution of Charles I. Some context on the role of radical Puritan factions in these events would be helpful.

quote: Some would say it’s the unnatural philosophers that are to blame for Protestants fighting Protestants in England and Catholics everywhere else.
subject: Religious conflict in Europe
note: This alludes to the broader context of religious wars and conflicts in Europe during the 17th and 18th centuries, including conflicts between different Protestant denominations and between Protestants and Catholics. Some context on the major religious conflicts of the era would be helpful.

quote: As when your own Judges finally went up to Salem and pointed out that the people there were going crazy.
subject: Salem Witch Trials
note: This refers to the Salem Witch Trials. Some context on the events, the accusations of witchcraft, and the eventual intervention of authorities would be helpful.

quote: Sir Isaac—who is the President of the Royal Society
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Isaac Newton was a highly influential physicist, mathematician, astronomer, alchemist, and natural philosopher. He was later President of the Royal Society.

quote: the King’s Arrow—going to build ships for the Navy
subject: King's Arrow
note: The King's Arrow (or Broad Arrow) was a mark used by the British government to indicate ownership of property, particularly timber reserved for shipbuilding. A brief explanation of its significance would be helpful.

quote: all that. He moves toward a talkative group that has formed on the center of the ferry’s deck, allowing the less erudite (for these must be Harvard men) to break the wind for them.
subject: Harvard College
note: Harvard College, founded in 1636, was one of the earliest colleges in the American colonies. Some context on its founding and early curriculum would be helpful.

quote: They guess that he has come on some Alchemical errand
subject: alchemy
note: Alchemy was a philosophical and protoscientific tradition practiced throughout Europe, Africa, and Asia. It aimed to purify, mature, and perfect certain objects.

quote: primum mobile
subject: Primum Mobile
note: Primum Mobile is a Medieval Latin term used in Ptolemaic astronomy to refer to the outermost moving sphere in the geocentric model of the universe. It was thought to be the source of motion for all other spheres.

quote: vis viva is conducted into Dr. Waterhouse’s gear-boxes
subject: vis viva
note: Vis viva is Latin for 'living force.' In early formulations of mechanics, it was a concept related to kinetic energy. Understanding its historical context in the development of physics is helpful.

quote: He’s not entirely satisfied with Harvard College then!?
subject: Harvard College
note: A brief overview of Harvard's founding and status in the late 17th/early 18th century would be useful. It was already a well-established institution, which makes Waterhouse's new venture seem more audacious.

quote: He is a Fellow of the Royal Society!
subject: Royal Society
note: The Royal Society was (and is) a highly influential scientific institution. Knowing its role and prestige provides context for the characters' reactions.

quote: fallen quite under the spell of Herr Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: Leibniz was a major figure in philosophy and mathematics. Understanding his rivalry with Newton and his philosophical ideas is important.

quote: him that stole the calculus from Sir Isaac
subject: calculus
note: This refers to the bitter dispute between Newton and Leibniz over the invention of calculus. Knowing the basics of this controversy is essential.

quote: notwithstanding Sir Isaac’s having exploded the old ways
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Newton's revolutionary impact on science is a key theme. A brief summary of his major contributions (physics, optics) would be helpful.

quote: Mill—designed after Leibniz’s principles—that he imagines will discover new truths through computation
subject: computing
note: This refers to Leibniz's ideas about mechanical computation and a 'universal characteristic'. Some background on early computing machines would be useful.

quote: Their colleagues are muttering to them words like “Hanover” and “Ansbach.”
subject: Hanover
note: This alludes to the impending Hanoverian succession in England. Knowing the basics of this political situation is important for understanding the characters' reactions.

quote: they are behaving as if Queen Anne were already dead
subject: Queen Anne
note: Queen Anne was the current monarch. Knowing that she was nearing the end of her reign and had no clear heir is crucial to understanding the political context.

quote: the peace of Westphalia did.
subject: Peace of Westphalia
note: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended the Thirty Years' War in the Holy Roman Empire and the Eighty Years' War between Spain and the Dutch Republic. It is often seen as a turning point in European history, establishing the modern state system.

quote: the removal of the King’s head from the rest of the King
subject: Charles I
note: This refers to the execution of King Charles I of England in 1649, a pivotal event in the English Civil War.

quote: Cromwell smashed the Royalists for the umpteenth and final time at Worcester.
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: Oliver Cromwell was a key figure in the English Civil War and later Lord Protector of England, Scotland, and Ireland. The Battle of Worcester in 1651 effectively ended Royalist hopes of restoring the monarchy at that time.

quote: Charles the Second ran off to Paris
subject: Charles II
note: Charles II fled to France after the Battle of Worcester. He lived in exile until the Restoration of the monarchy in 1660.

quote: the Dutch Republic, of course.
subject: Dutch Republic
note: The Dutch Republic (c. 1581-1795) was a major European power during the 17th century, known for its trade, naval strength, and relative religious tolerance.

quote: a Mr. Huygens in the Hague.
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: Christiaan Huygens (1629-1695) was a prominent Dutch physicist, mathematician, astronomer and inventor. He made significant contributions to optics, mechanics, and horology (timekeeping).

quote: the Navigation Act.
subject: Navigation Acts
note: The Navigation Acts were a series of laws passed by the English Parliament to restrict colonial trade to England. They were designed to benefit English shipping and commerce and were a major source of tension with the Dutch Republic.

quote: Rich Tories, no less!
subject: Tories
note: The term 'Tory' emerged in English politics during the late 17th century to describe those who supported the monarchy and the Church of England, as opposed to the Whigs, who favored parliamentary power and religious toleration.

quote: the Royal Society?
subject: Royal Society
note: The Royal Society of London for Improving Natural Knowledge is a learned society and the United Kingdom's national academy of sciences. Founded in 1660, it played a key role in the development of modern science.

quote: before Natural Philosophy as we know it.
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: Natural philosophy was the term used to describe the study of nature in the pre-modern era, encompassing what we now call science. It emphasized observation, experimentation, and reasoning to understand the physical world.

quote: Francis Bacon, Galileo, Descartes
subject: Francis Bacon
note: Francis Bacon (1561-1626) was an English philosopher and statesman who advocated for the scientific method and empirical observation. He is considered one of the founders of modern science.

quote: Alchemy.
subject: alchemy
note: Alchemy was a philosophical and protoscientific tradition practiced throughout Europe, Africa, and Asia. Alchemists sought to transmute base metals into gold, discover a universal elixir, and achieve spiritual enlightenment.

quote: Monsieur LeFebure, who was the apothecary to the French Court.
subject: Nicolas Le Febvre
note: Nicolas Le Febvre (c. 1610-1669) was a French chemist and apothecary who served as the royal apothecary to Louis XIV. He was a proponent of iatrochemistry, which sought to apply chemical principles to medicine.

quote: Louis the Fourteenth. His formal coronation was in 1654.
subject: Louis XIV
note: Louis XIV (1638-1715), known as the Sun King, was King of France from 1643 until his death. His reign was marked by absolute monarchy, cultural flourishing, and numerous wars.

quote: They anointed him with angel-balm, a thousand years old.
subject: Holy Ampulla
note: The Holy Ampulla (Sainte Ampoule) was a glass vial containing holy oil used to anoint the kings of France during their coronation. Legend held that it was brought from heaven by a dove for the coronation of Clovis I in the 5th century.

quote: John Wilkins
subject: John Wilkins
note: John Wilkins (1614-1672) was an English clergyman, natural philosopher, and writer. He was a founding member of the Royal Society and a proponent of scientific inquiry and religious toleration.

quote: Cromwell and his New Model Army
subject: New Model Army
note: The New Model Army was a key force in the English Civil War, instrumental in Oliver Cromwell's rise to power. Understanding its role helps contextualize the political landscape of the time.

quote: Oxford, meaning only to pay a call on John Wilkins
subject: John Wilkins
note: John Wilkins was a prominent figure in the scientific revolution, a founder of the Royal Society, and an important influence on many scientists. Knowing his role provides context for his association with other figures mentioned.

quote: he was Warden of Wadham College, which is part of the University of Oxford
subject: Wadham College
note: Wadham College, Oxford, was a center of scientific and philosophical inquiry during this period. Knowing its reputation helps understand why Wilkins was there and what kind of work he was doing.

quote: Natural Philosophy
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: Natural Philosophy is the historical term for what we now call science. Understanding this helps avoid anachronistic interpretations.

quote: Christopher Wren, Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Robert Hooke was a significant scientist and inventor. Knowing his contributions to fields like microscopy and physics provides context for his presence in this group.

quote: Experimental Philosophical Clubb
subject: Royal Society
note: The Experimental Philosophical Clubb was an early precursor to the Royal Society. Understanding the Royal Society's role in promoting scientific inquiry is important.

quote: Mr. Clarke in Grantham
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Mr. Clarke is a reference to Isaac Newton. Knowing Newton's significance as a scientist is crucial for understanding the context of the story.

quote: table of Huygens’s house in the Hague
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: Christiaan Huygens was a prominent Dutch scientist known for his work in optics, mechanics, and astronomy. His invention of the pendulum clock was a major advancement.

quote: Galileo had timed his experiments
subject: Galileo Galilei
note: Galileo Galilei was a key figure in the scientific revolution, known for his work in astronomy, physics, and mathematics. Understanding his contributions provides context for the discussion of scientific progress.
